title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a university student called Hannah talking about a rooftop gardening project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a university student called Hannah talking about a rooftop gardening project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A university student called Hannah talks about helping with a rooftop garden.
extracts.audio_script: Hi, I’m Hannah and I volunteer at Sky Garden, a food-growing project above a large building in the city centre. People often imagine that the garden is on top of a hotel or an office building, but it is actually on the roof of a [9]shopping centre[/9]. Shoppers can see signs for it, although access is only possible with a volunteer or during public events. The space was once empty and covered in grey concrete. Now it contains raised beds, flowers and seating areas with views across the city.||I joined the project while looking for something practical to do alongside my university course. I am studying business, not science, so I worried I would know less than everyone else. However, beginners are welcomed warmly. Our most successful crop last year was not tomatoes, as I expected, but [10]salad leaves[/10], which grew quickly and could be picked several times. Much of the food goes to a community café downstairs. New volunteers attend a short introductory meeting, where the main instruction is how to use the roof's [11]watering system[/11], since water must be distributed carefully in containers above a building.||Jobs change with the seasons. In my first month, I did not plant vegetables at all. My first regular task was [12]mixing compost[/12], combining material in the correct amounts before it was added to beds. It was messy but satisfying. Volunteers do not need gardening tools because these are supplied. On bright days, there is very little shade on the roof, so the organiser always tells newcomers to bring [13]sun cream[/13], as well as drinking water. Gloves are available to borrow, and sessions are cancelled if conditions become unsafe in strong wind.||The team tries to avoid waste. Food scraps from the café are processed in special containers and rain is collected from a roof structure. We also use lightweight planting containers made from [14]recycled plastic[/14], because heavy traditional pots would not be suitable for the roof. The garden is not simply for growing food; it also supports wildlife in the city. This spring volunteers built a small [15]insect hotel[/15], providing places for bees and other helpful insects to shelter. Children attending open days have found this particularly interesting.||I have learned far more than I expected. The optional workshop that attracted most volunteers this year was about [16]herb growing[/16], as people wanted ideas suitable for their own balconies or window boxes. Regular volunteers who complete a season are offered a free [17]cookery class[/17] at the café, using produce grown above it. This year we are hoping to expand our seating area so older visitors can enjoy the garden more comfortably. To fund this, our autumn event will be a [18]soup festival[/18], where visitors can sample several recipes and donate to the project. Joining Sky Garden has made me see city spaces very differently, and I strongly recommend it to anyone interested.

dialogue.speaker: hannah
dialogue.text: Hi, I’m Hannah and I volunteer at Sky Garden, a food-growing project above a large building in the city centre. People often imagine that the garden is on top of a hotel or an office building, but it is actually on the roof of a shopping centre. Shoppers can see signs for it, although access is only possible with a volunteer or during public events. The space was once empty and covered in grey concrete. Now it contains raised beds, flowers and seating areas with views across the city.||I joined the project while looking for something practical to do alongside my university course. I am studying business, not science, so I worried I would know less than everyone else. However, beginners are welcomed warmly. Our most successful crop last year was not tomatoes, as I expected, but salad leaves, which grew quickly and could be picked several times. Much of the food goes to a community café downstairs. New volunteers attend a short introductory meeting, where the main instruction is how to use the roof's watering system, since water must be distributed carefully in containers above a building.||Jobs change with the seasons. In my first month, I did not plant vegetables at all. My first regular task was mixing compost, combining material in the correct amounts before it was added to beds. It was messy but satisfying. Volunteers do not need gardening tools because these are supplied. On bright days, there is very little shade on the roof, so the organiser always tells newcomers to bring sun cream, as well as drinking water. Gloves are available to borrow, and sessions are cancelled if conditions become unsafe in strong wind.||The team tries to avoid waste. Food scraps from the café are processed in special containers and rain is collected from a roof structure. We also use lightweight planting containers made from recycled plastic, because heavy traditional pots would not be suitable for the roof. The garden is not simply for growing food; it also supports wildlife in the city. This spring volunteers built a small insect hotel, providing places for bees and other helpful insects to shelter. Children attending open days have found this particularly interesting.||I have learned far more than I expected. The optional workshop that attracted most volunteers this year was about herb growing, as people wanted ideas suitable for their own balconies or window boxes. Regular volunteers who complete a season are offered a free cookery class at the café, using produce grown above it. This year we are hoping to expand our seating area so older visitors can enjoy the garden more comfortably. To fund this, our autumn event will be a soup festival, where visitors can sample several recipes and donate to the project. Joining Sky Garden has made me see city spaces very differently, and I strongly recommend it to anyone interested.
